stories.body: U.S. Central Command said American forces shot down four Iranian drones aimed at maritime traffic near the Strait of Hormuz and then struck radar and surveillance sites on Goruk and Qeshm Island, escalating a conflict that began with joint U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran in February and has since spread to Lebanon, Gaza, northern Israel and Kuwait. President Donald Trump told NBC the strikes have left Iran with roughly 21–22% of its missiles, yet Tehran continues to demand sanctions relief, access to oil revenues and leverage over the strait as conditions for any interim deal, while Hezbollah maintains attacks against Israeli forces in southern Lebanon under Iranian backing.
stories.headline: U.S. strikes Iranian coastal radar after drones threaten Hormuz shipping

stories.body: Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi told Lebanon’s Al Mayadeen that any U.S.-Iran ceasefire must include a halt to Israel’s operations in southern Lebanon and a withdrawal of Israeli forces, reiterating Tehran’s support for Hezbollah as the group continues cross-border attacks first launched in March in solidarity with Iran. The stance complicates U.S. efforts to craft an interim regional peace while maritime tensions worsen—U.S. forces recently boarded an oil tanker they accused of aiding Iran, Iran’s navy fired warning shots at U.S. destroyers, and drone attacks temporarily shut Oman’s Mina al Fahal terminal—against a backdrop of curtailed Hormuz shipping, rising oil prices and growing humanitarian concerns flagged by the U.N. World Food Programme.
stories.headline: Iran links wider ceasefire to Hezbollah front in Lebanon

stories.body: Kyiv authorities reported multiple fires, including in a nine-story apartment block in the Podil district and in cars hit by debris in Obolon, after Russia carried out a heavy pre-dawn bombardment on June 2, prompting renewed air-raid warnings and calls from President Volodymyr Zelenskiy for residents to heed alerts amid intelligence reports of a possible massive strike. Russia had warned of systematic attacks on military and decision-making centers in Kyiv in retaliation for a deadly drone strike in Russian-occupied Luhansk, which Ukraine denies, while Kyiv’s mayor Vitali Klitschko said emergency services were battling blazes across the capital.
stories.headline: Kyiv reels from fresh Russian missile barrage

stories.body: Ousmane Sonko, recently ousted as prime minister but still president of the majority Pastef party, said after meeting President Bassirou Diomaye Faye that disagreements over the party’s executive role mean Pastef will not join the new government, intensifying political turmoil as the country faces a misreported-debt scandal that froze a $1.8 billion IMF program. Lawmakers had reinstated Sonko as an MP and elected him speaker after Faye dissolved the government and named economist Ahmadou Al Aminou Lo as premier, while Senegal races to renegotiate with the IMF before June 30 to address debt that has reached 132% of GDP.
stories.headline: Senegal’s Pastef quits governing coalition amid debt crisis

stories.body: The European Union and China issued their first joint statement in seven years agreeing to launch three months of ministerial-level trade and investment consultations to address a €360 billion deficit that the EU says is growing by €1 billion a day, following weeks of tariff threats and warnings about a “China Shock 2.0.” EU trade commissioner Maroš Šefčovič and Chinese commerce minister Wang Wentao will focus on rebalancing trade, export controls, intellectual property and WTO reforms, with a joint monitoring system to flag trade surges, while Brussels weighs future quotas on hybrids and chemicals after earlier electric-vehicle tariffs failed to curb imports.
stories.headline: EU and China enter three-month talks to tame €360bn trade gap

stories.body: South African authorities deployed police units and military assets across cities such as Johannesburg ahead of June 30 demonstrations by anti-immigration groups like March and March, whose leaders insist on peaceful protests but have demanded undocumented foreigners leave the country, prompting many migrants—documented and undocumented—to fear harassment after weeks of vigilante intimidation and five reported killings of Mozambican nationals. With foreign-born residents comprising about 4% of the population yet blamed for crime and joblessness amid high unemployment, several African governments have repatriated citizens, while President Cyril Ramaphosa condemned xenophobia and migrant advocates warned of a repeat of deadly 2008 and 2021 unrest.
stories.headline: South Africa braces for anti-migrant marches with heavy security

stories.body: In a 6–3 ruling, the U.S. Supreme Court struck down President Donald Trump’s executive order that sought to deny citizenship to children born in the country to parents who are in the United States illegally or temporarily, with Chief Justice John Roberts’ majority opinion affirming the 14th Amendment’s guarantee and Justice Brett Kavanaugh concurring on statutory grounds while Justices Clarence Thomas, Samuel Alito and Neil Gorsuch dissented. The decision, which Trump called “too bad for our Country,” leaves any change to birthright citizenship requiring a constitutional amendment and came after lower courts blocked the order and after Trump personally attended the April arguments.
stories.headline: Supreme Court upholds birthright citizenship, rejecting Trump order

stories.body: The Supreme Court, in a 6–3 decision authored by Justice Brett Kavanaugh, invalidated federal caps on coordinated spending between candidates and political parties, arguing the limits were a severe First Amendment burden and that stronger parties could counterbalance super PACs, while Democrats warned the ruling favors big donors who can write large checks to party committees. The case, brought by the NRSC and J.D. Vance’s 2022 Senate campaign after the Trump Justice Department refused to defend the law, immediately allows parties—able to accept $44,300 per donor annually versus $3,500 for candidates—to direct unlimited funds alongside campaigns, likely shifting TV ad spending and reshaping the midterm landscape.
stories.headline: Court ends limits on party coordination with campaigns

stories.body: The Senate voted 52–47 to approve a Republican bill granting the Department of Homeland Security an additional $70 billion for Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol operations, rejecting Democratic efforts to permanently ban a $1.8 billion “anti-weaponization” fund that Trump wants to compensate allies who claim government mistreatment despite Justice Department assurances it will not proceed. Several Republicans from battleground states backed a failed Democratic motion to kill the fund, while later GOP amendments to scrap or repurpose it also fell short, leaving the House to consider the bill next week as Republicans defend Trump’s broader deportation crackdown ahead of the midterms.
stories.headline: Senate passes $70 billion ICE funding while Trump ‘anti-weaponization’ fund survives

stories.body: A sprawling heat dome pushed temperatures to dangerous levels across the Midwest and Great Lakes on June 30, with forecasters warning the system will expand into the eastern U.S. ahead of the July Fourth weekend as cities opened cooling centers, shelters offered relief to unhoused residents and even animal shelters improvised splash pools for pets. Images from Detroit, Washington, Jackson, Mississippi, and Lake Michigan beaches showed crowds seeking shade and water, while the Associated Press noted the heat coincides with World Cup knockout rounds and is expected to strain infrastructure as humidity and overnight temperatures remain high.
stories.headline: Heat dome scorches Midwest as East braces for dangerous temperatures

stories.body: Justice Democrats-backed challenger Melat Kiros defeated 15-term Rep. Diana DeGette in Colorado’s 1st District Democratic primary, toppling a 68-year-old incumbent who had held the seat since 1997 despite outspending Kiros nearly three-to-one and receiving late support from progressive House leaders. Kiros, endorsed by Sen. Bernie Sanders and fresh off a wave of insurgent left victories that ousted New York incumbents last week, framed her campaign as a generational break with corporate-aligned Democrats, warning that she would not back Leader Hakeem Jeffries if he continues accepting corporate PAC money, a signal that the party’s left flank could complicate caucus unity if Democrats win a narrow House majority.
stories.headline: Democratic socialist Melat Kiros unseats Rep. Diana DeGette in Denver

stories.body: Spain’s Supreme Court is considering asking the Court of Justice of the European Union whether the government’s April decree regularizing thousands of undocumented migrants conflicts with the EU Migration and Asylum Pact, after the autonomous governments of Aragón and Valencia challenged the measure that would integrate irregular residents into the tax and social security system. The contentious-administrative chamber has asked both regional governments and the State Attorney’s Office if a preliminary reference should be filed to clarify the decree’s compatibility with EU law as the case proceeds.
stories.headline: Supreme Court weighs EU query on Spain’s migrant regularization decree

stories.body: A Spanish congressional committee approved the report on Sumar’s bill to grant Spanish nationality to Sahrawis, a move framed as settling a historic debt after Spain’s withdrawal from Western Sahara, with votes from the PSOE and other leftist parties, Junts abstaining, and PP and Vox opposing. The measure, nearly two and a half years in the making, now heads toward a full vote despite right-wing resistance, aiming to formalize citizenship access for Sahrawis as part of Spain’s reassessment of its colonial-era responsibilities.
stories.headline: Congress advances Spanish citizenship bill for Sahrawis over PP and Vox objections

stories.body: Popular Party leader Juanma Moreno failed to win the first investiture vote for Andalusian president after Vox withheld support, saying ongoing negotiations show willingness but not yet sufficient guarantees, while left-wing parties opposed the pact and accused the PP of hiding concessions. Moreno insisted a PP-Vox agreement is the only viable government and vowed to keep negotiating before the second vote in 48 hours, as PSOE leader María Jesús Montero warned that partnering with Vox undermines the PP’s moderation narrative and Vox leader Manuel Gavira criticized the PP for delaying a deal.
stories.headline: Vox blocks Juanma Moreno’s first investiture vote in Andalusia

stories.body: The Generalitat presented a new rail services plan aiming to double Catalonia’s ridership to one million daily trips by 2040 through three phases that add 27.8 million train-kilometers, 182 new trains, 419 drivers and 266 conductors while reorganizing Rodalies, Regionals and Avant services with higher frequencies, semidirect routes and major transfer hubs in Lleida, Sant Vicenç de Calders, Sants and Maçanet. Early actions slated for 2027–2028 include extending the R1 to Cerdanyola Universitat, launching semidirect R2 services, opening an R-Aeroport line and stretching the RT2 to Vilafranca, with later milestones such as doubling R8 frequency, creating an R88, lengthening Avant offerings, completing the R3 double-tracking and starting an orbital R9.
stories.headline: Catalonia unveils rail service plan to double daily trips by 2040

stories.body: Barcelona will host the Grand Départ of the 2026 Tour de France on July 2, 4 and 5, prompting the city to impose extensive street closures, traffic detours and public-transport changes expected to affect roughly 850,000 visitors amid peak summer tourism; bus routes will face diversions or suspensions while metro service is reinforced and race circuits become impassable except at designated crossings. City authorities are providing live updates on mobility impacts as they prepare for three days of congestion tied to the opening stages of the race.
stories.headline: Barcelona warns of major mobility disruptions during Tour de France Grand Départ

stories.body: A KPMG study commissioned by Hutchison Ports found the Port of Barcelona’s semi-automated BEST container terminal produced €2.195 billion in gross value added in 2025—equivalent to 0.7% of Catalonia’s GDP—while supporting 20,571 jobs across its value chain as throughput tripled to 2.8 million TEU over the past decade. The report highlights €876 million in investments, new quay cranes, plans for six additional automated blocks and expanded rail access via the future Nou Llobregat complex, underscoring BEST’s role in generating about 42% of the port’s value added and strengthening intermodal freight links.
stories.headline: BEST container terminal generates 0.7% of Catalonia’s GDP
